Tracing the epistolary networks of Roman provincial governors during the Crisis of the Third Century — how correspondence maintained imperial cohesion amid political fragmentation and rapid succession.
outline_hint: Title slide: 'Letters of Loyalty — Epistolary Networks and Imperial Cohesion, 235–284 CE'
Historical context: the Crisis of the Third Century — succession instability, provincial secession, external threats
Sources and methodology: Pliny the Younger's letters as model, the Augustan correspondence archive, papyrological evidence
Network mapping: Mermaid diagram of governor-to-emperor and governor-to-governor letter routes across key provinces
Case study 1: Gaul and Britannia — Postumus' breakaway and the correspondence gap
Case study 2: Palmyra — Odaenathus and the diplomatic letter as substitute for military deployment
Quantifying fidelity: formulaic loyalty language in official dispatches (KaTeX table of frequency distributions)
The cursus publicus under stress — infrastructure of imperial communication and its breakdown
Argument synthesis: epistolary practice as a technology of governance, not merely record-keeping
Conclusions and open questions — toward a network-theoretic model of Roman imperial resilience
